title: Importance-weighted drift detection — stop nuking the crash model when unimportant features drift

observation.problem: 89.2% of features (141/158) are flagged as drifting, triggering 'critical' severity. This reduces crash model signal weight to 20% — effectively disabling it. But the drifted features are mostly momentum (mom_1m, mom_6m, mom_12m) and short-term volatility (vol_1m, vol_3m) which naturally shift with market conditions.
observation.root_cause: The drift detector treats all features equally when computing drift_pct. If 8 low-importance momentum features drift but the 2 most predictive macro features (ICSA, NFCI, yield curve) are stable, drift_pct is still 80% and severity is 'critical'.
observation.impact: The crash model's predictive power is wasted. The signal engine runs at 20% crash weight even when the model's important features are still in-distribution.

what_i_built.summary: Importance-weighted drift scoring that weights each feature's drift flag by its crash model importance. When important features are stable, effective severity is lower — the model retains more signal.

components.name: importance_weighted_drift()
components.description: New method in DriftDetector that normalizes feature importances to sum to 1, then computes weighted drift % instead of raw count. Features the model relies on count more.
components.example: Raw drift: 89% (141/158 features). If those 141 features only carry 30% of model importance, weighted drift is 30% — 'moderate' instead of 'critical'.

components.name: drift_direction attribution
components.description: Each feature now reports mean_shift (positive = shifted higher, negative = lower) and spread_change (>1 = wider/more volatile, <1 = compressed). Helps diagnose WHY features drifted.
components.example: mom_1m: mean_shift=+0.15, spread_change=1.3 (momentum shifted up and became more volatile)

components.name: stable_important_features
components.description: List of important features that are NOT drifting, sorted by importance. Shows which features the model can still rely on.
components.example: ['yield_curve_10y_3m', 'ICSA_4w_chg', 'NFCI'] — the model's top macro features are stable

components.name: effective_severity
components.description: New field in drift reports. Uses importance-weighted severity when importances are provided, falls back to raw severity otherwise. All routers now use effective_severity for signal weight multiplier.
components.example: raw_severity='critical', effective_severity='moderate' → crash model runs at 70% weight instead of 20%

test_classes: TestImportanceWeightedDrift (5 tests) — core weighted drift computation
TestDriftDirection (4 tests) — mean shift and spread change
TestBackwardCompatibility (2 tests) — no importances = same behavior

before.description: 89% of features flagged as drifting. Crash model essentially disabled at 20% weight.

after.importance_weighted_drift_pct: Depends on model feature importances. In synthetic tests: 8/10 low-importance features drifting → raw 80%, weighted <20%
after.effective_severity: Can be 'low' or 'moderate' when important features are stable, even if raw severity is 'critical'
after.description: Crash model retains signal when its important features are stable. Raw drift tracking unchanged for monitoring.

strengths: Backward compatible: no importances = same behavior as before
Both raw and weighted severity exposed in API for transparency
Drift direction helps diagnose why features shifted
Stable important features list shows what the model can still rely on
All 4 routers updated consistently (market, crash, stock, drift)
limitations: Actual impact depends on what the trained crash model's feature importances look like. If the top features are also drifting, weighted severity won't be lower.
Feature importances are based on LightGBM gain — not necessarily the best proxy for which features the model is 'sensitive to'. SHAP values would be better but more expensive.
Importance-weighted severity thresholds (0/15/35/65%) are slightly relaxed compared to raw thresholds (0/10/30/60%) — this is intentional but may need tuning with real data.

self_critique: The real test is running this against the actual crash model's feature importances. In synthetic tests, the weighted drift clearly differentiates important vs unimportant feature drift. But I haven't verified what the actual crash_model.pkl's top features are and whether they're among the 17 stable ones. The next cycle should run the full pipeline and compare raw vs weighted severity on live data.
next_steps: Run live drift check with crash model importances to see actual weighted severity
Add SHAP-based importance weighting as alternative to LightGBM gain
Consider feature-group-level drift (macro group, momentum group, volatility group) for better interpretability
Add drift trend tracking: is drift increasing or decreasing over time?
Frontend: show both raw and weighted drift in the dashboard
